[IMAGE:0] ...until February, that is. 

It's a little shy of midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of Alexandra David-Neel, French occultist, Theosophist, Buddhist, professional opera singer, explorer, adventurer, and first-rate author, whose actual exploits make those of fictional characters like Indiana Jones look lame, and who almost made her 101st birthday. Ladies, if you want a role model, you could do worse. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you. See you the first Monday in February!***

Dear JMG
Apologies in advance for length.
I got a copy of The Art & Practice of Geomancy just before discovering your upcoming publication of The Sacred Geometry Oracle (which I have pre-ordered as it particularly resonates); but, given I have some pressing questions right now, thought it made sense to try using the A&PofG rather than wait for the SGO

Background: Trying to decide what to do next to earn a living

A current project on the go but not sure how much longer it will continue, and two potential projects to either replace or develop alongside the existing project, I am trying to get an insight into what to work on and plan for.

Using shield chart and trying to formulate yes/no questions as a start point to exploring possibilities (i.e. that the first set of 4 divinations is a broad picture to guide subsequent questions).
Not sure if questions are focussed enough or need more thought.

Four divinations (4 questions) were done one following the other over a 2 hr period. They are interconnected questions with an underlying 'feeling' that it is necessary to move to action in short order.
Question 1 asks about existing project
Question 2 asks about potential project A
Question 3 asks about potential project B
Question 4 asks about alternative projects

Divination 1 - (Project X - the existing project)
Question 1: Will [X] continue to provide enough work to support us for the next 12 months?

Judge: Amissio
LW: Cauda draconis
RW: Rubeus
N1: Aquisitio
N2: Tristitia
N3: Laetitia
N4: Conjunctio
D1: Fortuna major
D2: Puella
D3: Via
D4: Carcer
M1: Caput draconis
M2: Albus
M3: Cauda draconis
M4: Via

First feeling is that this is plainly a 'no' answer.

If that is correct, then are there any subtleties in the reading that might help interpret?

If Cauda draconis is 'ending/completion' and Amissio is 'loss', what might Rubeus as right witness signify?

Is the way of points relevant here? From Judge Amissio to LW Cauda Draconis to N3 Laetitia to D2 Puella

I attempted to establish a useful mind state and connection and ask the question neutrally.
The feeling is that this confirms that 'current stage' is coming to a close. Gut feeling is that the work is nearly done and it is time to move on, which is fine, but it does add a certain 'interest' to getting moving on something else!

Divination 2 - (Project A – a potential to supercede project X)

Judge: Fortuna minor
LW: Cauda draconis
RW: Rubeus

N1: Amissio
N2: Laetitia
N3: Amissio

D2: Fortuna majority
D3: Rubeus
D4: Populus

M1: Rubeus
M2: Cauda draconis
M3: Laetitia
M4: Populus

This one has me puzzled – on page 63 in the divinatory meaning for Fortuna minor it says 'favourable whenever you want to proceed quickly' which would tie in with the need to do something as an existing project looks to wrap up; then, Cauda draconis showing as left witness shows [p.60] as 'it is favourable in any situation where something is coming to an end' [existing project X from divination 1?], but unfavourable for most other questions'.

Right witnesses Rubeus (1st divination) and Albus (2nd divintation) have me wondering what I am bringing to this party!

If I have it correct, the way of points on this:
Fortuna minor to Cauda draconis to Amissio to Fortuna minor

Do you think the reading might suggest that decisive and intent action will work in favour because of the question's relation to first divination of something ending, or is it suggesting the idea is a lemon [or success but only short-lived]?

Of course, the literal answers to the specific questions might overlook the fact that a combination of projects might work… and short-lived success is not necessarily a failure, just a way-marker!

Give me another year or so and I might start getting a handle on this!

Any thoughts or pointers on what I am missing much appreciated.

Thank you,

Earthworm

replies.content:
This morning I awoke with an idea – what if my start point of asking questions about work related things was based on starting from a false premise?

Concern about what is going to happen can easily become a mind-trap of fear (fear of the unknown) and attempting to get definitive answers is just a way of smoothing out worry of not knowing what is going to happen or what it might be best to do?

The use of a system of divination at such a simple level (shield chart, yes/no answers) attempts to simplify things and reduce to simple binary options. Binary options allow for no subtlety and the world is such that complexity is not going to be well served – it is rare that life can be simplified to the mundanity of yes/no particularly if you explore the idea of 'Okay, I have found two options, now, what is a third option?'

Amissio is clearly complex in that it can mean loss in terms of losing something you want, but also loss in terms of losing something you do not want.

What about Amissio in terms of letting go?

Being trapped in only a concern about earning a living misses the greater question of what actually might be useful. What we think we need and what we actually might need could be very different. If the starting point of what to do only considers a narrow blinkered perspective, then we cut ourselves off from other vistas and possibilities.

In asking question 4 (Should I consider a completely different project outside of the 3 projects I am looking at?) the answer seemed to be a clear-cut 'No', or as JMG put it:
“It's not far from "Oh for the gods sakes, will you just stop?"”

So then I asked Q5: Am I asking the correct questions?”
And the answer seemed to be a very solid 'Yes'

How to encompass the two?
Because of Rubeus appearing as RW for the querent in both Q1 & Q2, JMG had said:
“You may need to approach the entire issue from a broader perspective.”

Slept on that and awoke with the idea which takes me back to the top of this writing and brings me back down through and once more to the question:

What about Amissio in terms of letting go?

Perhaps one of the possibilities for interpreting Q4 is that I should just let go of trying to fix fine detail in attempting to determine exactly which or what project I should pursue.
That things are not as simple as that.
That I know what subjects interest me and reducing those to 'income' is like putting on blinkers and moving only within the material.

Answer to Q5 suggests I am asking the correct questions but Q4 tells me to let it go.
Perhaps let go of what is actually fear of the unknown in not knowing how I will earn an income – that there is more going on here than meets the eye and that, in fact, I know what I need to be working on and the questions relating to will this earn me an income are not very useful.

Amissio is telling me to let go of what seem like big concerns but are actually minor concerns, and get on with what is useful and needful to do.

Of course, that means I now have another question to ask through divination:
“Is it useful for me to practice this form of divination?”

comments.title: Some questions and data points
comments.content:
In this picture
Hu Gadarn is depicted with what looks like an oar in his left hand. What is in his right hand? Is it a small plow? Some kind of musical instrument?

In the Orphic hymns as translated by Thomas Taylor, Taylor indicates the pronunciation of the name Proserpine as rhyming with vine, for instance: “Prolific, venerable, Nurse divine, thy daughter loving, holy Proserpine”. Was he wrong? I always thought Proserpine was pronounced “Pra-sir-PEE-nay” or “Pra-sir-PEE-nuh”.

For the TSW files: 2019 has been the best year of my life. I strongly believe my chipper mood, sanguine outlook, and especially lately, my good fortune are owed to doing the Sphere of Protection (slightly modified AODA style with the Druid pantheon) once a day without fail for the last two years. 2019 began for me with my favorite aunt dying, and of course this wasn’t a happy event, however, I was able to rescue her two cats which I believe helped her move on to her future existences. After she passed away, it came down to a question of her cats either being rescued by someone or dying of freezing/dehydration in an abandoned house. It was my daily Druid practices that gave me the necessary guts and detachment from the normal, materialistic grind to go in there and get them. The cats are now happily living with my parents and can’t get enough of being petted, pampered, and spoiled. They will live out the rest of their lives in luxury and comfort. My aunt’s spirit thanked me many times over and the experience has brought me much closer to both my patron gods and other gods.

The year also brought me some uncanny successes in fields I had all but given up upon. Every time I made a small act of sacrifice or act of kindness, monetary or otherwise, it seemed to be returned to me by a factor of exponents. This is still ongoing.

Recently, my friend’s mother passed away and once again, because of my SoP, meditations, and Druidry in general, I was able to be a comforting and helpful presence. I wasn’t confused or upset like most people in this culture are when they’re around death, and I think this helped both my friend and his mom.

In short, JMG, I owe you a great deal for re-introducing me to occultism and beyond it, religion. I will always be grateful that you helped me to get through my own thick, formerly atheist skull these past few years. Thank you with all my heart.

replies.title: Re: Some questions and data points
replies.content:
1) It's a plough. Hu Gadarn in legend is (a) the one who led the Welsh from Deffrobani in the distant east to Britain by boat, and (b) the first person to plow the soil of Britain. That's more than an agricultural myth, of course; the draft animals he yoked to pull the plow were the Two Calves of the Spotted Cow, that latter being the starry heavens; his plowing is the precession of the equinoxes, with the two equinoxes being the calves in question.

2) Ah, I see you haven't yet encountered the execrable British pronunciation of Latin. "Proserpine" rhyming with "mine" belongs to the same abuse of Latin as "Caesar" rhyming with "squeezer."

3) You're most welcome and thank you!

replies.title: Re: Some questions and data points
replies.content: Is Hu Gadarn a thoughtform created by Druid Revival tradition?

replies.title: Re: Some questions and data points
replies.content: Whether the Druid Revival created Hu Gadarn or whether Hu Gadarn created the Druid Revival is an interesting question to which we don't know the answer. New deities appear from time to time, and find names and imagery appropriate to their nature; how that happens is something that deities understand a lot better than we do.

replies.title: Re: Some questions and data points
replies.content: So how are you supposed to pronounce Caesar?

replies.title: Re: Some questions and data points
replies.content:
There is no letter J in Latin -- it's always I, pronounced Y. C was pronounced like K, never like S, and AE rhymed with "pie" or "lye," not with "squee." Back when he was still walking around, in other words, his full name was more or less KAY-us YOOL-yoos KYE-sar.

You're welcome.

replies.title: Re: Some questions and data points
replies.content: The only problem is, 99.999% of people will say “You mean JU-lee-us SEE-zer?”

replies.title: Re: Some questions and data points
replies.content: Oh, I know. When you're talking to people who don't know Latin it's necessary to use the English or American pronunciation. I'd just like there to be more people who know that's not the way the Romans said it.

comments.title: Incorporeal-accessible Games?
comments.content:
Bit of an off the wall question for JMG and Commentariat:

I've been interacting with a friendly spirit of a playful persuasion as of late. It has an interest in playing games such as chess, and I've been trying to, for instance, make a move and then pray to the spirit asking what move it wants to make. Unfortunately, we're running up against the issue that in prayer ease of communication is proportionate to the parties' openness to each other, and a competitive game by necessity introduces friction between the players. Not only is it a problem that I have to break my focus on my moves to open myself to receive the spirit's next move, but when I get in a bind I start finding myself thinking "they couldn't have really meant that move [because then the spirit would win ;) ]" which is naturally frustrating to all involved.

It occurs to me that divination is, from a certain point of view, a means to allow immaterial forces to communicate by material means, which got me wondering:

1) Have you come across any competitive or cooperative games which were intentionally designed to facilitate incorporeal players?

2) Have you heard of any instances of a divination system being used to allow an incorporeal entity to play an off-the-shelf game?

3) You've mentioned before that different divination systems have different personalities. If you were to design a game involving incorporeal players of good faith, is there an existing divination system you would think would be suited to providing the link to the incorporeal player?

4) I seem to recall the problem with Ouija boards is that they in essence lack magical security and could see a similar potential for problems here. If I were to homebrew an incorporeally-accessible game, would ensuring the space is banished before and after every play session and requiring each player to be willing, of good faith, and named (preferably with titles) be enough to ensure proper spiritual hygiene?
4b) Are there any other spiritual health and safety considerations?
4c) Can you think of any other general ritual considerations that might be good to keep in mind? For instance, I could see a need to isolate the material player from the oracle to give the incorporeal player a fair chance and multiple incorporeal players need to be isolated from each other.

replies.title: Re: Incorporeal-accessible Games?
replies.content:
1) The Golden Dawn game of Enochian chess is specifically set up for that.

2) Not off hand, but someone's probably done it.

3) That depends so much on the details of what you want to do that there's no single answer for it.

4a) It would probably be better to do it in a space that's not just banished, but opened by a more extensive opening ritual -- you can find one of those in most of my books on magic.

4b) Check the results closely against what actually happens to see if it's telling the truth.

4c) You can't isolate incorporeal players from each other -- they exist on a plane of being you can't control. This is one of those situations where an "as if" approach works perfectly well.

comments.title: Moon void of course
comments.content:
In the process of studying lunar days to time my magical operations, I’ve come across the Void of Course periods. Websites I’ve referred to indicate that anything started during a period in which the moon the void of course will yield no results.

I had not yet come across the concept, I was wondering whether it is a concept only present in Vedic astrology, and what considerations should be taken of it in timing of magical operations such as ceremonial magic operations?

replies.title: Re: Moon void of course
replies.content:
Okay, void of course Moons are one of the two grand bugbears of modern popular astrology -- the other is of course Mercury retrogrades. It's not just Vedic, but it's an indication in certain branches of astrology that has been blown vastly out of proportion.

For the benefit of other readers, I'll mention that the moon is void of course when it has completed its last major aspect with a planet before it passes into another sign. In horary astrology, it's an important indicator: it means that nothing will come of the question for which the chart is cast. In some branches of electional astrology, including those that govern Renaissance-style astrological magic, the same thing applies. Ceremonial magic of the Golden Dawn variety, as far as I can tell, isn't affected by it at all, and neither are most other magical practices.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Just under a decade ago, a small manufacturing business I had started in the UK, finally crashed and burned after several years of struggle on my part trying to keep it going. There were many reasons for this, but although it was largely due to my own mistakes one contributing factor was the substantial number of regulations and record keeping requirements that I had to obey. The time and money spent dealing with this could have been put to better use trying to keep things going.

Business failures are always painful and one of the aspects I found especially hard to deal with was the impact on the many people who depended on me or those to whom the business owed money. I did what I could to protect them but in the end it was little enough. There have been worse events in my life but this is high, very high, on that short list.

I mentioned this in the Ecosophia blog and you suggested I write an article on what it was like dealing with the regulatory aspects of running a business in the UK. As it happens - the last decade has been a pretty transformative one for me on an interior level - based on many of the things you have written in that period. I take your comment very seriously. Writing on almost any subject that I have an opinion on is not a problem but this is one that not only hurts but still has some real potential to damage me and mine.

Finally, this is occurring just as I'm about to take the final step of embedding myself in a particular part of the country for the long term and concentrating on non-professional matters.

So I did a geomantic divination. I've been making divinations of this nature for a couple of years but about three months ago started doing them regularly after a gap in practice. I've had some success at this (which is personality shattering news all by itself I might add but it really has been a decade of change).

I did a house chart on the question:

"Shall I write about my experience starting and running a small business?"

I got:

1. Populus
2. Carcer
3. Via
4. Amissio
5. Caput Draconis
6. Albus
7. Fortuna Major
8. Caput Draconis
9. Carcer
10. Aquisto
11. Aquisito
12. Rubeus

My assumption, and I have some doubt about this, is that the writing of an article in a 9th house matter on the grounds that any writing is an art form. It's not done with the intention of making a profit so not a 2nd house matter which would have been my choice I I were doing this in the hope of writing a best seller.

Populus is certainly me in this instance, passive, undoubtedly a mixture of motivations, and subject to forces outside of myself. The chart itself does perfect by the unfavourable Carcer in 2 and 9.

So I've ended up with a chart that perfects by conjunction with an unusually unfavourable figure. I understand that Carcer represents both restriction and stability but I'm too close to the problem to really draw a conclusion.

Is it "Don't touch this with a barge pole - you will be jailed", I know that's catastrophising but that's my state of mind at the moment. As I said, this event is up there on that short list.

An alternative interpretation is "Do this, it's really going to hurt and there will be be an immediate loss (possibly of guilt), but in the long term you will gain some good fortune and profit and there will be some kind of stability". That's based on a glance at the cardinals as well.

So the bottom line is: Perfection by unfavourable figures?

I've not calculated the witnesses or judge as it's a house chart and if you have a moment I'd appreciate a view on doing that for house charts as well. I've seen you comment that this is not typically done, but I've also seen house charts where this has been done and the three figures placed in the middle.

Andy

Perfection by a very unfavorable figure, btw, generally amounts to "you can do this, but you'll be sorry you did." So it amounts to the same thing either way.

As for the judge and witnesses, I've found that they tend to confuse the matter in house charts more than they help, so I leave them off. Other people have different opinions, and your mileage may vary.

Hi JMG and everyone,

I started posting the beginning of my German version of the SOP as described in the "Druid Magic Handbook" a while ago. Some pauses and restarts due to life interruptions later I finally got to the seventh gate and would like to post my complete version:


Erstes Tor

Beim Maifalken im Morgenwind und dem großen Namen HU rufe ich die Luft, ihre Götter, ihre Geister und ihre Kräfte. Ich bitte die Luft um ihren Segen.
Ich danke der Luft für ihre Gaben.
Und mit Hilfe der Luft banne ich in und um mir alle ihre unausgewogenen Aspekte. Ich verbanne sie weit weg von mir.


Zweites Tor

Beim weißen Hirschen im Sommerlicht und dem großen Namen SUL (gesprochen: SÜL) rufe ich das Feuer, seine Götter, seine Geister und seine Kräfte. Ich bitte das Feuer um seinen Segen.
Ich danke dem Feuer für seine Gaben.
Und mit Hilfe des Feuers banne ich in und um mir alle seine unausgewogenen Aspekte. Ich verbanne sie weit weg von mir.


Drittes Tor

Beim weisen Lachs im heiligen Teich und dem großen Namen ESUS (gesprochen: ESÜS) rufe ich das Wasser, seine Götter, seine Geister und seine Kräfte. Ich bitte das Wasser um seinen Segen.
Ich danke dem Wasser für seine Gaben.
Und mit Hilfe des Wassers banne ich in und um mir alle seine unausgewogenen Aspekte. Ich verbanne sie weit weg von mir.

Viertes Tor

Beim großen Bären, der die Sternenhimmel bewacht, und dem großen Namen ELEN rufe ich die Erde, ihre Götter, ihre Geister und ihre Kräfte. Ich bitte die Erde um ihren Segen.
Ich danke der Erde für ihre Gaben.
Und mit Hilfe der Erde banne ich in und um mir alle ihre unausgewogenen Aspekte. Ich verbanne sie weit weg von mir.

Fünftes Tor

Beim leuchtenden Herzen der Mutter Erde und dem großen Namen KED rufe ich den Geist in der Tiefe, seine Götter, seine Geister und seine Kräfte. Ich bitte den Geist in der Tiefe um seinen Segen.
Ich danke dem Geist in der Tiefe für seine Gaben.

Sechstes Tor

Bei der Sonne in ihrer Herrlichkeit, Quelle des Lichts, und dem großen Namen KELI rufe ich den Geist in der Höhe, seine Götter, seine Geister und seine Kräfte. Ich bitte den Geist in der Höhe um seinen Segen.
Ich danke dem Geist in der Höhe für seine Gaben.

Siebtes Tor

Bei den sechs Mächten hier gerufen und hier versammelt und dem Großen Wort AWEN (gesprochen: AUEN) rufe ich den Geist in mir. Mögen sich das Universum in mir und das Universum um mir im Gleichgewicht befinden.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello Archdruid,

Is there such a thing as "Grand Unified Theory of Magic" which can explain the inner mechanism of all the variety of magical practices around the world?

I think Neoplatonism is a good candidate, but I don't have enough knowledge and experience to give a definitive statement about this.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I am inclined to think, after many decades studying magic with an eye to that very question, that no self-consistent "grand unified theory of magic" can possibly be constructed. The differences between, say, the magical traditions in the Graeco-Roman world (and its cultural descendants), on the one hand, and the magical practices of, say the Australian Indigenous population, or the shamanic practices of the various Northern Eurasian peoples, or the practices of the various Native American tribes, or the enormous variety of magical practices found in Africa, are simply too great, and rest on too divergent foundations in the experiences of the peoples in question.

One of the reasons that "grand unified theories" of things in the material world can be constructed, is that the material world appears to exhibit internal self-consistency. But the material world looks like a very small, limited and impoverished creation when compared to the very rich immaterial worlds that some people can glimpse faintly when one or another of their immaterial senses wake up briefly. I suspect the logical self-consistency of the material world is one of the severe limitations deliberately built into its design as a safety meassure.

Magic seems to me, after long reflection, not to be a non-sentient "thing" which can be mastered, but some odd sort of thoroughly sentient, immaterial, living being whom one must court. And, just as among us humans, so too with magic, no one method of courtship will work on every person one might desire, nor will it work equally well in everyone's hands. One's own personal strategy of mundane courtship must be devised with one eye on who and what one actually is, and the other eye on the character, desires and proclivities of the person one wishes to court. So it is, too, with magic ... at least, that's how it seems to me.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There have been many attempts to construct a grand unified theory of magic, and they all contradict one another. Robert M. may well be correct that magic is a being who must be courted rather than a thing that can be understood and controlled; what I know for certain, though, is that every proposed unified theory of magic simply mirrors back the prejudices of the person who creates or believes it.

As I see it, the universe is not a rational place, for the simple reason that "rationality" means no more than the set of mental processes that evolution has hardwired into the brains of a particular species of social primate on a small planet in an unremarkable corner of a very big cosmos. The universe does what it does; our brains are not big enough to understand more than a small fraction of what it does; and -- well, to borrow a metaphor I used in a different context, trying to create a theory to explain something as richly complex as magic is like trying to use a child's six inch long ruler to measure a galaxy.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I certainly agree that the universe is not a rational place, for exactly the same reasons that our host cited.


PS Also, I realized after I sent my earlier comment (where I said that magic seems to me to be an immaterial, sentient being who has to be courted rather than controlled) that my words might seem to impute a distinct individuality and maybe even some sort of personality to that sentient being whom we call "magic."

I didn't intend to imply any such thing. In the realm of such a being, individuality is not the same sort of thing as it is with us humans. In that realm, such a being can be said both "to be X" and "not to be X" (for any value of "X"), even though the two statements are logically incompatible for us humans. Two manifestations of such a being can also be said with equal truth "to be one and the same being" and "to be two different and distinct beings."

The Neoplatonist Iamblichus touches, somewhat obliquely, on this when he writes, referring more narrowly just to the Gods:

"In the first place, therefore, you say, 'It must be granted that there are Gods.' Thus to speak, however, is not right on this subject. For an innate knowledge of the Gods is coexistent with our very essence ; and this knowledge is superior to all judgment and deliberate choice, and subsists prior to reason and demonstration." (De mysteriis, I.3)

comments.title: Clarification on a long-told story
comments.content:
Almost two years ago, over at Ecosophia, I told my story about getting hagged on a dark night in January of '18. Our conversation was moving along nicely until I tossed out a comment about the smell of this demon/spirit, how it was nasty, bloody-metallic, absolutely alien, and VINEGARY.

Well you dropped the thread like a hot potato leaving me wondering what I had said wrong. Not long after that I started picking up on your advice to leave a bowl of vinegar out in the bedroom to PREVENT non-physical entities like the Old Hag from condensing. Ah, that's why...

How could my perception of that nasty smell have been so wrong? I wondered. Well, turns out it wasn't. My current magical training is really helping me with my memory and enriching my understanding of HOW things like this work. And this is a good example.

Our cabin reeked of vinegar, and no mistake, because there were 2 full carboys of apple cider fermenting in the corner by the wood stove. I had never had one go off before, turn to vinegar, but for some reason both of these - all 10 gallons - had done just that.

And the vinegar smell was late in the sequence. I smelled the bloody-metallic nasty alien smell first and then the vinegar smell slowly REPLACED it as the Hag dissipated. I can only assume that a friendly entity drew the vinegar vapors out of the carboys as a weapon against the Hag. My wife thinks it was our "brownie" friend, and it is interesting that that whole story occured just a few months before this happened, and we do seem to have passed a major test of good faith with it. But if the cider spoilage was intentional, a preparation for this event, we would be talking about a being who exists outside of time I think. And wouldn't a house fay be constrained in time by its etheric body?

Talking about this with my very Baptist parents over Christmas, they immediately twisted it to an attempt by God to get my attention. From my perspective, though, He missed a world-class chance of doing just that by using roundabout magical methods to accomplish what for a Supreme Deity might require the equivalent of snapping his fingers. Buzz! Wrong answer! At least for the God-trying-to-get-my-attention argument.

(I've since discovered through geomantic divination that the Hag was summoned and sent to attack me by one of the 2 people I hate in this world. A solid cut-and-clear working has now thoroughly severed that connection.)

Anyway, it's great to be able to get at the magical logic behind something like this. All of this was so utterly foreign when I started. That's all your doing, btw. Your books, your blogs, your community, it's all absolutely priceless.

Thank you for this.
Tripp

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've just come across a fascinating Persian-Arabic-Turkish geomantic miscellany of 66 folios dating probably to the 16th-century Ottoman context, with additions (medical and political prognoses) dated to the late 18th. Unlike the other geomantic texts I've worked on, however, which were made by and for courtly and scholarly elites, this one is quite déclassé, with plenty of grammatical and spelling mistakes, and featuring simpler, more rough-and-ready applications, while still being fully astrological -- in short, a relatively rare mirror onto popular Ottoman geomantic practice over several centuries.

The thing is, this miscellany also contains a large table, in Persian, of the questions appropriate to the geomantic houses that is both more extensive and rawer than any I've seen before, with each house being assigned several surprising questions that don't seem to match the astrological remit of that house, and certainly not the list you give in your manual. (Interestingly, it assigns talismanry to the ninth house along with astrology and geomancy, and oneiromancy to the eighth house.) And stranger still, it also assigns a full range of questions to houses 13-16, which I haven't seen before. Have you? Or would this seem to be a new and perhaps peculiarly Ottoman take on the science? And do you happen to know which, if any, of the Latinate geomantic manuals include similar tables of house questions I might compare this with?

Just to give you a taste of the weirdness of some of the questions is this Persian table, here's the full list of them for houses 1 and 13-16 -- wild nah?


House 1:

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That makes a great deal of sense. In some geomantic writings the right witness is treated as the 13th house, left witness as 14th, judge as 15th, and reconciler as 16th, and it would be a logical extension of that end of geomancy to assign specific questions to those houses. The only challenge would be figuring out how to manage modes of perfection, and that's not that difficult -- simply assume that these four fit between the 12th and 1st, and Bob's your uncle.

I don't recall any Western geomantic treatise that has anything like so extensive a list of house attributions, though Robert Fludd's as yet untranslated books of geomancy come close.

Is there any chance you can translate this and get it into circulation in some form? I'd be most interested to study it!

comments.title: castsing geomantic charts in a ritualized way
comments.content:
A few weeks ago I posted the question on drawing the house chart for a geomantic reading in a ritualized way:
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/74930.html?thread=6714034#cmt6714034

In a follow-up question I asked whether you think this practice still falls under the common rules for safety around children regarding divination. Your answer was, that a small ritual like this should not be any problem around children.

I was, however, concerned since the chart I cast asking whether the way of ritualized drawing I proposed was a good idea showed - despite giving an overall yes to my question - Rubeus in the 5th house. So I decided to cast a few more charts to sort that out. I feel that beside the answer I got to my questions this process was important for me for some reason and I'd like to share my experiences here.

First, I asked whether the casting the chart in the ritualized way would be ok for my children and I got:

1 cauda draconis - 2 puella - 3 fortuna minor - 4 carcer - 5 via - 6 amissio - 7 fortuna minor - 8 acquisitio - 9 acquisitio - 10 acquisitio - 11 acquisitio - 12 carcer - rw populus, lw fortuna minor, j fortuna minor

So basically a yes, however the translation made by carcer (isolation) in the 12th (limitations) and 4th (real estate) seemed to suggest that some kind of special separation might be necessary. I was still unsure and asked whether it was ok cast the charts when I'm alone in a room where my children do not spend much time. This time I got rubeus in the 1st house and denial of perfection. I cast a second chart with a slightly refined question and got a clear response that I should not do this kind of divination when my children are at home.

At this time I was afraid of annoying the oracle but kept on asking, namely "Is it ok then if I cast charts in a ritualized manner when my children are not at home or when I am not at home?" and I got

1 cauda draconis - 2 laetitia - 3 populus - 4 carcer - 5 puer - 6 laetitia - 7 laetitia - 8 tristitia - 9 conjunctio - 10 carcer - 11 acquisitio - 12 carcer - rw via, lw fortuna minor, j fortuna major

Which is now an amazingly clear answer that even doubtful-me understands - I hope ;-) ... if you take the relavant houses as 5 (children), 7 (wife), 8 (magic), 9 (divination) you get positive connections between all houses involved and a favourable figure in the 8th house. Additionally, the carcer-translation from the first chart shows up again in exactly the same way as in the first chart.

To sum up, I am very grateful since I really feared to annoy not only myself with my everlasting uncertainty. I read the last chart as an "Yes, yes, YES!" and will stop asking on this issue as cauda draconis in the first might suggest...

Have a good start into 2020!
Nachtgurke

comments.title: Magical experimentation
comments.content: Suppose you wanted to discover the magical correspondences of materials for which there is no traditional information. How would you structure the experiment?

replies.title: Re: Magical experimentation
replies.content: Oh, it would take more than a single experiment. You'd start by finding out any folklore that exists about the material, and also studying the ecological role of the material and its biological source (if it has one). You'd then have to get enough of a grasp of the traditional Doctrine of Signatures to be able to hypothesize a planetary, zodiacal, and elemental correspondence. Having done so, you'd test that out using ritual workings to see if in fact the material resonates with those correspondences -- and that presupposes that you have sufficient skill in ritual to tell the difference. That said, it's work that's well worth doing, and I hope you get to work on it.

replies.title: Re: Magical experimentation
replies.content:
Not the OP, but Doctrine of Signatures?

All in all, it sounds like it'll take years of study just for one material....

replies.title: Re: Magical experimentation
replies.content: That's the traditional medieval and Renaissance system of figuring out the correspondences of an animal, plant, or mineral from its physical form and behavior.

Archdruid,

What is the Ars Notaria?

replies.title: Re: Learning
replies.content: Notoria, not Notaria. It's a branch of magic that uses incantations and focused concentration on diagrams to produce the equivalent of a talent for some field of knowledge -- for example, you could use the incantation and diagram for astrology to prime your mind so that astrological concepts make intuitive sense and learning astrology is very easy and quick for you. It's one of the least known and least understood branches of magic, and somebody's going to need to do a lot of original work and experimentation with the old Ars Notoria texts to figure out how it works and get it back into circulation in the modern occult community.

replies.title: Re: Vinegar v Salt
replies.content:
Vinegar fumes have a fiery quality, and if you tend to imbalance that way anyway vinegar might overset you further. Salt has an earthy quality and so will counter that.

The difference between the two is that vinegar actively interferes with the condensation of dense ethers; salt simply absorbs dense ethers. It's like the difference between bug spray and flypaper.

comments.title: Denial of perfection with favourable judge
comments.content:
Thank you for Magic Monday.

When interpreting geomagnetic charts cast for Yes/No questions, and the resulting chart lacks perfection but yet has a favourable judge. How would you interpret the answer?

replies.title: Re: Denial of perfection with favourable judge
replies.content:
First of all, this is why you should turn off your spell check. I assume you mean geomantic charts, not geomagnetic charts!

Second, it's crucial when doing geomancy to decide before you cast the chart whether you're going to interpret it as a shield chart or as a house chart. If you choose to do a shield chart, the judge and witnesses give you the answer. If you choose to do a house chart, the presence or absence of the mode of perfection gives you the answer. If you try to interpret both at once you'll just confuse things, until you have enough experience to know how to fit them together.

In response to your specific chart, the answer is no, your income will not increase this year. It'll do so in future years -- Fortuna Major can mean difficulty at the beginning and success thereafter -- and you'll be okay in the meantime.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
1) Last week I asked about my cellphone and how I find it distinctly unpleasant even if it's off. You said that it still transmitted, and I decided to check (I know newer models do, but mine is an old flip phone). I have the gear to check for transmissions, and so I turned it on and off, and found it doesn't transmit while off.

Do you know why I would find a non-transmitting cell phone unpleasant?

2) I have a friend who's doing analysis to try to figure out why vinyl records sound better than CDs. She's informed me that by every metric she can use to look at them, they should be the same, but she can hear the difference. This has me wondering if there's an etheric component to music, which would also explain why the fey are so often associated with otherworldly music. Do you know if music has an etheric component, and if so, if vinyl records also create that etheric component?

replies.title: Cell phones, and vinyl records
replies.content:
A cell phone can have unpleasant associations with the sense that someone just might call you at any minute. It looks the same, whether on or off.

Musical instruments produce sounds that go beyond human hearing (ultrasonic). If they aren't totally suppressed by a low-pass filter (which has no apparent effect, when tested in isolation), they can be mathematically "reflected" by the digital mastering process back into the audible range, where they are not harmonious. (See "aliasing" on Wikipedia.) So, a CD can perfectly represent the sounds that we want to hear, yet create new sounds which interfere. Analog recording processes don't do that. The aliased components manifest themselves near the upper end of the audio range, where they are difficult to detect. Here's a discussion, with demo video. https://theproaudiofiles.com/digital-audio-aliasing/

Lathechuck

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Nope. Clearly you do, though, so go with that.

2) Of course music has an etheric component, but I don't think that's involved in this case. Any digitalization process breaks up the flow of sound into a set of discrete numbers -- it's like the difference between a ramp and a staircase. Even if the steps are very small, they're there, and even though the digitally minded insist that shouldn't matter, it does.

Spirit Within: Moon?

As there are several possible correspondence categories (and I know there's likely no one "right way") and I don't feel at all confident about asking them where they wish to be, I was hoping you wouldn't mind saying how you would assign them so that I can quit mulling over it and just get started! I asked here a few weeks ago about a name for Earth's intelligence and since there isn't one, would Gaia work for Spirit Below?

Thank you as always!!

replies.title: Re: Planetary intelligences SoP
replies.content:
The traditional assignments of the planets to the SoP are as follows:

East: Sun
West: Mercury
South: Mars
North: Venus
Below: Saturn
Above: Jupiter
Within: Moon

I'd say give that a try and see how it works for you.
